edges.source: FLNA Deficiency and Enteric Neuron Development Failure

edges.target: FLNA Deficiency and Enteric Neuron Development Failure

nodes.description: Loss-of-function FLNA mutations disrupt the N-terminal region of filamin A, which is crucial for proper enteric neuron development. A frameshift mutation between the two initial methionines of FLNA allows translation of a truncated protein from the second methionine, producing a filamin A protein with abnormal cytoskeletal actin organization. This impairs enteric nervous system development and function, leading to intestinal dysmotility and pseudo-obstruction.
nodes.id: FLNA Deficiency and Enteric Neuron Development Failure

evidence.snippet: one affected male from a large CIIPX-affected kindred bears a 2-bp deletion in exon 2 of the FLNA gene
the filamin protein is present in the patient's lymphoblastoid cell line that shows abnormal cytoskeletal actin organization compared with normal lymphoblasts
the filamin N terminal region between the initial two methionines is crucial for proper enteric neuron development
Filamin A is mutated in X-linked chronic idiopathic intestinal pseudo-obstruction with central nervous system involvement.
The two remaining families were shown to have intragenic duplications of FLNA only.

nodes.description: Defective enteric neuron development leads to impaired intestinal motility, presenting as chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction with episodes mimicking mechanical bowel obstruction without physical blockage. Bladder dysfunction can co-occur.

evidence.snippet: We propose that duplication of FLNA may contribute to the bowel and bladder phenotype seen in these seven families.
